2/14
INTRODUCTION

Introduction to Linear Models

Linear models are widely used in practice and have been studied extensively. They make predictions using a linear function.

1/12
SLIDE 04

Theory of Linear Models

Linear models are a class of models that make predictions using a linear function. They have been studied extensively in the last few decades, with roots going back over a hundred years. Linear models can only model linear relationships, which are lines in the case of a single feature.

📄 p.59, p.234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-12. Comparing coefficient magnitudes for ridge regression with different values — Page 65
2/12
SLIDE 05

Implementation of Linear Models

We can apply the LogisticRegression and LinearSVC models to the forge dataset. Linear models can be used for regression, and the coefficients and intercept can be found using different methods.

📄 p.70
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-14. Comparing coefficient magnitudes for lasso regression with different values — Page 69
3/12
SLIDE 06

Binning, Discretization, and Linear Models

Binning and discretization can be used with linear models. Linear models can build a more complex model of the data when combined with binning and discretization.

📄 p.234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-1. Comparing linear regression and a decision tree on the wave dataset — Page 235
4/12
SLIDE 07

Implementation of Binning and Discretization

We can build a new linear regression model and a new decision tree model on the one-hot-encoded data. The result is visible when we compare the models.

📄 p.237
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-2. Comparing linear regression and decision tree regression on binned features — Page 237
5/12
SLIDE 08

Univariate Nonlinear Transformations

Univariate nonlinear transformations can help linear models for regression. Adding squared or cubed features can help linear models.

📄 p.246
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-7: In[34]: — Page 247
6/12
SLIDE 09

Implementation of Univariate Nonlinear Transformations

Finding the transformation that works best for each combination of dataset and model is somewhat of an art. We can try different transformations and compare the results.

📄 p.249
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-8. Histogram of feature values for X[0] after logarithmic transformation — Page 249
7/12
SLIDE 10

Parameters of Univariate Nonlinear Transformations

Univariate nonlinear transformations have parameters and tradeoffs. The choice of transformation can affect the model's performance.

📄 p.246
8/12
SLIDE 11

Univariate Statistics

Univariate statistics can influence how models perform on a given dataset. Binning, polynomials, and interactions can have a huge influence on model performance.

📄 p.250
9/12
SLIDE 12

Implementation of Univariate Statistics

We can add noninformative noise features to the data and expect the feature selection to identify the features that are noninformative and remove them.

📄 p.251
10/12
SLIDE 13

Model-Based Feature Selection

Model-based feature selection can be used to select the most important features. Linear models have coefficients that can be used to capture feature importances.

📄 p.253
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-34. Feature importances computed from a random forest that was fit to the — Page 101
11/12
✦

Summary of Linear Models

Linear models are widely used and can be improved with binning, discretization, and univariate nonlinear transformations. Feature selection is also important for model performance.

Linear models
12/12
